mn20:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Sāvatthī, in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s park.
mn20:2.1: “Bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* engaged in the higher mind [*adhicitta*] should from time to time bring five *nimittas* to mind.
mn20:3.1: Here, bhikkhus, when, because of some *nimitta*, as a *bhikkhu* attends to some *nimitta*, bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] arise — thoughts [*vitakkā*] connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — then that *bhikkhu*, bhikkhus, should attend to another *nimitta*, connected with what is skillful.
mn20:3.2: As he attends to another *nimitta*, connected with what is skillful, those bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — are abandoned, they disappear.
mn20:3.3: With their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself becomes steady inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi hoti*], and settles into *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn20:3.4: Suppose, bhikkhus, a skilled carpenter or a carpenter’s apprentice were to hammer out, draw out, and dislodge a coarse peg with a fine peg;
mn20:3.5: even so, bhikkhus, when, because of some *nimitta*, as a *bhikkhu* attends to some *nimitta*, bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] arise — thoughts [*vitakkā*] connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — then that *bhikkhu*, bhikkhus, should attend to another *nimitta*, connected with what is skillful.
mn20:3.6: As he attends to another *nimitta*, connected with what is skillful, those bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — are abandoned, they disappear.
mn20:3.7: With their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself becomes steady inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi hoti*], and settles into *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn20:4.1: But if, bhikkhus, while that *bhikkhu* is attending to another *nimitta*, connected with what is skillful, there still arise bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — then that *bhikkhu*, bhikkhus, should examine the drawbacks of those thoughts [*vitakkā*]:
mn20:4.2: ‘These thoughts [*vitakkā*] are unskillful, these thoughts [*vitakkā*] are blameworthy, these thoughts [*vitakkā*] have *dukkha* as their result.’
mn20:4.3: As he examines the drawbacks of those thoughts [*vitakkā*], those bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — are abandoned, they disappear.
mn20:4.4: With their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself becomes steady inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi hoti*], and settles into *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn20:4.5: Suppose, bhikkhus, a woman or a man, young, youthful, and fond of adornment, had the carcass of a snake, or the carcass of a dog, or the carcass of a human being hung around the neck: they would be appalled, ashamed, and disgusted;
mn20:4.6: even so, bhikkhus, if, while that *bhikkhu* is attending to another *nimitta*, connected with what is skillful, there still arise bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — then that *bhikkhu*, bhikkhus, should examine the drawbacks of those thoughts [*vitakkā*]:
mn20:4.7: ‘These thoughts [*vitakkā*] are unskillful, these thoughts [*vitakkā*] are blameworthy, these thoughts [*vitakkā*] have *dukkha* as their result.’
mn20:4.8: As he examines the drawbacks of those thoughts [*vitakkā*], those bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — are abandoned, they disappear.
mn20:4.9: With their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself becomes steady inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi hoti*], and settles into *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn20:5.1: But if, bhikkhus, while that *bhikkhu* is examining the drawbacks even of those thoughts [*vitakkā*], there still arise bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — then that *bhikkhu*, bhikkhus, should resort to not paying attention to those thoughts [*vitakkā*].
mn20:5.2: As he resorts to not paying attention to those thoughts [*vitakkā*], those bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — are abandoned, they disappear.
mn20:5.3: With their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself becomes steady inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi hoti*], and settles into *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn20:5.4: Suppose, bhikkhus, a person with good eyesight wanted not to see forms that had come within range;
mn20:5.5: he would close his eyes or look away elsewhere;
mn20:5.6: even so, bhikkhus, if, while that *bhikkhu* is examining the drawbacks even of those thoughts [*vitakkā*], there still arise bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — they are abandoned, they disappear.
mn20:5.7: With their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself becomes steady inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi hoti*], and settles into *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn20:6.1: But if, bhikkhus, while that *bhikkhu* is resorting to not paying attention to those thoughts [*vitakkā*], there still arise bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — then that *bhikkhu*, bhikkhus, should attend to the settling of the *saṅkhāras* of thought [*vitakkasaṅkhārasaṇṭhānaṁ*].
mn20:6.11: What if I were to stand instead?’
mn20:6.15: What if I were to sit down instead?’
mn20:6.19: What if I were to lie down instead?’
mn20:6.2: As he attends to the settling of the *saṅkhāras* of thought [*vitakkasaṅkhārasaṇṭhānaṁ*], those bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — are abandoned, they disappear.
mn20:6.21: In that way, bhikkhus, that person, by giving up coarser and coarser postures, would adopt subtler and subtler postures.
mn20:6.22: Even so, bhikkhus, if, while that *bhikkhu* is resorting to not paying attention to those thoughts [*vitakkā*], there still arise bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — they are abandoned, they disappear.
mn20:6.23: With their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself becomes steady inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi hoti*], and settles into *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn20:6.3: With their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself becomes steady inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi hoti*], and settles into *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn20:6.4: Suppose, bhikkhus, a person were walking quickly.
mn20:6.7: What if I were to walk slowly instead?’
mn20:7.1: But if, bhikkhus, while that *bhikkhu* is attending to the settling of the *saṅkhāras* of thought [*vitakkasaṅkhārasaṇṭhānaṁ*], there still arise bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion —
mn20:7.2: then that *bhikkhu*, bhikkhus, with teeth clenched against teeth and the tongue pressed against the palate, should restrain, squeeze, and subdue the mind [*citta*] with mind.
mn20:7.3: As he, with teeth clenched against teeth and the tongue pressed against the palate, restrains, squeezes, and subdues the mind [*citta*] with mind, those bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — are abandoned, they disappear.
mn20:7.4: With their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself becomes steady inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi hoti*], and settles into *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn20:7.5: Suppose, bhikkhus, a strong person were to seize a weaker person by the head or throat or shoulders and restrain, squeeze, and subdue him;
mn20:7.6: even so, bhikkhus, if, while that *bhikkhu* is attending to the settling of the *saṅkhāras* of thought [*vitakkasaṅkhārasaṇṭhānaṁ*], there still arise bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion —
mn20:7.7: then that *bhikkhu*, bhikkhus, with teeth clenched against teeth and the tongue pressed against the palate, should restrain, squeeze, and subdue the mind [*citta*] with mind.
mn20:7.8: As he, with teeth clenched against teeth and the tongue pressed against the palate, restrains, squeezes, and subdues the mind [*citta*] with mind, those bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — are abandoned, they disappear.
mn20:7.9: With their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself becomes steady inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi hoti*], and settles into *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn20:8.1: When, bhikkhus, because of some *nimitta*, as a *bhikkhu* attends to some *nimitta*, there arise bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — and, as he attends to another *nimitta*, connected with what is skillful, those bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — are abandoned, they disappear;
mn20:8.10: With their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself becomes steady inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi hoti*], and settles into *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn20:8.11: This is called, bhikkhus, a *bhikkhu* who is master of the pathways of thought [*vitakkapariyāyapathesu*].
mn20:8.12: Whatever thought [*vitakkaṁ*] he wishes, that thought [*vitakkaṁ*] he will think [*vitakkessati*]; whatever thought [*vitakkaṁ*] he does not wish, that thought [*vitakkaṁ*] he will not think [*vitakkessati*].
mn20:8.13: He has cut off *taṇhā*, turned back the fetter, and by rightly fully understanding conceit has made an end of *dukkha*.”
mn20:8.15: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* delighted in the *Bhagavā*’s words.
mn20:8.16: The tenth, The Settling of Thoughts [*Vitakkasaṇṭhāna*], is finished.
mn20:8.17: The second chapter, the Lion’s Roar Chapter, is finished.
mn20:8.2: with their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself becomes steady inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi hoti*], and settles into *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn20:8.3: As he examines the drawbacks even of those thoughts [*vitakkā*], those bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — are abandoned, they disappear.
mn20:8.4: With their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself becomes steady inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi hoti*], and settles into *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn20:8.5: As he resorts to not paying attention to those thoughts [*vitakkā*], those bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — are abandoned, they disappear.
mn20:8.6: With their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself becomes steady inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi hoti*], and settles into *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn20:8.7: As he attends to the settling of the *saṅkhāras* of thought [*vitakkasaṅkhārasaṇṭhānaṁ*], those bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — are abandoned, they disappear.
mn20:8.8: With their abandoning, the mind [*citta*] itself becomes steady inwardly, settles down, becomes unified [*ekodi hoti*], and settles into *samādhi* [*samādhiyati*].
mn20:8.9: As, with teeth clenched against teeth and the tongue pressed against the palate, he restrains, squeezes, and subdues the mind [*citta*] with mind, those bad unskillful thoughts [*vitakkā*] — connected with desire, connected with aversion, and connected with delusion — are abandoned, they disappear.
mn20:9.1: The Shorter Lion’s Roar, the Hair-Raising, the Excellent,
mn20:9.2: The Greater and Lesser Mass of *dukkha*, and the Inference sutta;
mn20:9.3: Barrenness, the Forest Path, the Honeyball, and the Twofold Thought [*dvidhāvitakka*],
mn20:9.4: Again, the chapter on the discussion of five *nimittas*.
